one_liner: A 24-year-old previously healthy backpacker presents with 1 week of malaise, low-grade fever, nausea, anorexia, and new-onset jaundice 4 weeks after returning from a trip to rural Guatemala.

stage2.hpi_extension: He traveled to Guatemala 6 weeks ago for a 3-week volunteer trip where he drank unboiled well water, ate raw oysters and ceviche from a street vendor, and had no hepatitis vaccinations prior to departure. Over the past week he has developed anorexia, nausea, malaise, dark tea-colored urine, and pale clay-colored stools. His girlfriend noted yellowing of his eyes yesterday. He denies IV drug use, tattoos, blood transfusions, unprotected sexual encounters, or acetaminophen overuse. Two other volunteers on his trip have reported similar symptoms.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student, rare alcohol use, never smoker, no IV drugs, monogamous heterosexual relationship
stage2.physical_exam: General: fatigued young man, scleral icterus prominent. HEENT: jaundiced sublingual mucosa. Lungs: clear. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm. Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly with liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin, no splenomegaly, no ascites, no caput medusae, no spider angiomata. Skin: diffuse jaundice of the trunk and extremities. Neurologic: alert and oriented, no asterixis.

lab_results.result: ALT 2,450 U/L (markedly elevated), AST 1,980 U/L (markedly elevated), Total bilirubin 8.4 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 180 U/L, albumin 4.0 g/dL, INR 1.1, creatinine 0.9. Glucose normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated aminotransferases (ALT > AST, both > 1,000) with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and preserved synthetic function (normal INR/albumin) are classic for acute viral hepatitis without fulminant failure.

lab_results.result: IgM anti-HAV: POSITIVE. IgG anti-HAV: positive. HBsAg: negative, anti-HBc IgM: negative, anti-HCV: negative. HCV RNA: not detected. HIV: negative. Monospot: negative. CMV IgM: negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Positive IgM anti-HAV is diagnostic of acute hepatitis A infection. Negative hepatitis B and C serologies exclude co-infection.

stage5.final_clue: With supportive care (hydration, rest, antiemetics), his transaminases down-trend over the following 4 weeks and he returns to baseline with full recovery. No chronic carrier state develops and he is now immune to reinfection.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hepatitis A classically presents as an acute, self-limited illness in an unvaccinated traveler or person exposed via contaminated food or water. A prodrome of fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea, and right upper quadrant pain is followed by jaundice, dark urine, and pale stools. Outbreaks occur with raw shellfish, contaminated water, and daycare centers. Children are often asymptomatic or have milder disease; adults tend to have more severe symptoms. Recovery is complete within 4-8 weeks in nearly all cases.
debrief.pathophysiology: Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is a small, non-enveloped, positive-sense single-stranded RNA picornavirus (genus Hepatovirus) transmitted via the fecal-oral route. After ingestion, the virus reaches the liver via the portal circulation, replicates in hepatocytes, and is shed into bile and stool. Hepatocyte injury is primarily immune-mediated (cytotoxic T cell response against infected hepatocytes) rather than directly cytopathic. The incubation period is 2-6 weeks. There is no chronic carrier state - HAV causes only acute infection and does not predispose to cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is supportive: rest, hydration, antiemetics, and avoidance of hepatotoxins (alcohol, acetaminophen). Fulminant hepatic failure is rare (< 1%) and warrants urgent transplant evaluation. Prevention is by hand hygiene, safe food and water practices, and vaccination. The inactivated HAV vaccine is recommended for all children and for travelers to endemic regions. Post-exposure prophylaxis within 2 weeks of exposure uses the vaccine (for healthy persons age 1-40) or immune globulin (for infants, immunocompromised, elderly, or chronic liver disease).
high_yield_pearls: HAV: naked (+) ssRNA picornavirus; fecal-oral transmission via contaminated water, raw shellfish, and daycare exposures.
'Hepatitis A is Acute and Alone' - no chronic carrier state, no cirrhosis, no HCC risk.
IgM anti-HAV confirms acute infection; IgG anti-HAV indicates prior infection or vaccination and provides lifelong immunity.
Classic lab pattern of acute viral hepatitis: ALT > AST with both in the thousands, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and normal to mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.
Post-exposure prophylaxis within 2 weeks of HAV exposure: vaccine for healthy persons 1-40, immune globulin for infants/immunocompromised/elderly/chronic liver disease.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) - Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Viral hepatitis serologic markers and Hepatitis A' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) - Microbiology chapter, 'Picornaviruses - Hepatitis A virus' section.
